RepositoryCollection ⓘ

Farms to Freeways

This data set was exported from an Omeka Repository as an example of a DataCrate. It contains the Collections and Items from the repository but does NOT have the exhibitions. The DOI resolves to an archive of the data elsewhere

name description datePublished license creator publisher
Alice Potter Alice Potter was born on 30th September, 1929, at Blacktown. Alice's parents owned a house on land which was later bought by Blacktown Workers Club. Alice married in 1954 and the couple lived with her parents for one year, before moving to Rooty Hill. 2015-12-01
Amelia Vincent Amelia Vincent was born in 1914, in Italy. Amelia's family migrated to Sydney in 1922, and moved to a new block of land in Blacktown a year later. The family ran a vineyard and Amelia's father worked as a bricklayer. 2015-12-01
Amy Jackson Amy Jackson was born on 18th July, 1916, at Penrith and lived her whole life in the Penrith and Emu Plains area. She married in 1943 and later moved to Cambridge Park. 2015-12-01

Audrey Watson Audrey Watson was born on 23rd August, 1922 at Sydney. Audrey moved to Penrith with her husband in 1946. The couple lived in a flat above a shop for a number of years before building a house at Emu Plains. Audrey ran the tea room at the Lewer's Bequest regional gallery and like most of the interviewees she was a member of volunteer organisations for many years. 2015-12-01
Betty Hargreaves Betty Hargreaves was born on 2nd January, 1918, and moved to Penrith with her family in 1936. Betty met her husband whilst working as a secretary for her father, a produce merchant. Betty started the Penrith Community Aid organisation, as well as a Women's refuge, and was subsequently awarded an Order of Australia for services to the community. 2015-12-01
Brenda Niccol Brenda Niccol was born on 20th April, 1920, at the home of her grandmother in Ballarat. She grew up in the Riverina area, on a block of land settled by her father after the first World War. When Brenda was 17, her mother died suddenly. The following year her father sent her to stay at a guest house in Emu Plains, and she later returned there to work as a waitress. Her husband's family owned the guest house and in 1945 Brenda moved there to raise her family and run the guest house with her husband. 2015-12-01
Clare Pfoeffer Claire Pfoeffer was born on 30th January, 1913, at Parramatta. Claire moved to Seven Hills when she was aged 10. After leaving school, Claire held a variety of secretarial jobs through her life, working for 28 years at a company named Australian Cream of Tartar as secretary to the Assistant Managing Director. 2015-12-01
Diana Reynolds Diana Reynolds was born on 24th January, 1929, at Penrith. Diana's father was one of two doctors in Penrith and the surgery was attached to the house on the corner of High and Evans Street. Diana attended the Australian College of Physical Education after leaving school and then taught for 6 years at Methodist Ladies' College at Burwood. After her sister married, Diana returned home to work for her father as a secretary. Dianna married in the early 1960s and lived in South Penrith with her husband, who was a bomb disposal technician in the Air Force. 2015-12-01
Doreen Scott Doreen Scott was born on 1st December, 1925, at Camden. Doreen grew up on a property at Luddenham, moving to Penrith when she married in 1944. Doreen ran Scott's Corner Shop in High Street, Penrith for many years; her husband Jim ran his auto workshop / service station on the same block and the family lived there as well. The family also owned and ran Penrith's Dungowan Theatre, later changing the name to the Avon Theatre. 2015-12-01
Edith Mason Edith Mason was born on the 7th August, 1920, at Dubbo. The family moved to Mt Druitt in April, 1927, after her father built a house for them there. Her father worked for the railway. Edith married in 1941 and the couple lived for a short time in Orange, before returning to live with and care for her husband's mother, who developed glaucoma. 2015-12-01
Edna Vidler Edna Vidler was born on 31st December, 1908, at Newtown, Sydney. Edna was one of ten children and the eldest girl in her family. The family moved to Blacktown when Edna was aged 12. Edna left school at age 15 and went to work as a tailor in Parramatta. She married in 1930, and sadly her husband was killed in an accident when she was pregnant with their fourth child. 2015-12-01
Eugenie Stapleton Eugenie Stapleton was born on 21st January, 1911, at Lambton, Newcastle. Eugenie moved to St. Marys with her husband after the Second World War ended. They bought a new house and settled there with their two children. Eugenie's husband, Len, was the first engineer employed by St Marys Council and was responsible for most of the subdivision of St Marys. The couple also formed the St Marys Historical Society and Eugenie authored a book titled "South Creek, St. Marys - From Village to City" which was published by the Council. 2015-12-01
Florence Gibbons Florence Gibbons was born on 11th September, 1908, at Regentville. Florence lived in Penrith for much of her life. After she married in 1935, she and her husband built a house two doors along from Florence's parents in Henry Street, Penrith. 2015-12-01
Heather Corr Heather Corr was born on 10th April, 1923, at Penrith. Heather's father was a bootmaker and musician. The family lived in Penrith, in the street now known as Cox Avenue, on a block of land owned by her mother's father. After leaving school at age 14, Heather attended comptometer college and then worked in Martin Place, Sydney, until she married in 1948. 2015-12-01
Iris Hanna Iris Hanna was born on 30th November, 1914, at Lismore. Her father ran a dairy farm at Lismore. Iris married in 1944 and moved to Plumpton at that time, to live on a large poultry farm with her new husband. Iris and her husband had seven children together. 2015-12-01
Joan Jeffery Joan Jeffrey was born on 21st June, 1926, at Mudgee. At age 8 she moved with her family to Blacktown, where her father worked as one of two local policemen. The family lived in the police station until the building was demolished to make way for Westpoint Blacktown Shopping Centre. 2015-12-01
Joy Willis Joy Willis was born on 5th February, 1926 at St. Marys. Joy attended school in St Marys and Penrith. After leaving school Joy worked for Manpower, who would place her at various locations doing 'war work', until the end of World War 2 in 1945. Joy married in 1947 and the couple built a new home on her father's land, next door to where Joy had grown up. 2015-12-01
Joyce McKelvey Joyce McKelvey was born on 16th August, 1918, at Blacktown. Joyce's father was a baker and the family lived in the main street of Blacktown with the bakery in the back of the residence. Joyce recalls seeing the cattle herded through the main street of Blacktown each week, as they were transported from the Flemington markets to be slaughtered at Riverstone Meatworks 2015-12-01
Joyce Moon Joyce Moon was born on 15th March, 1921, at Beelbangera, near Griffith in NSW. Her father worked on Burrinjuck Dam before buying a poultry farm at Schofields, when Joyce was aged 2. Joyce married at age 22 and moved to Riverstone with her husband. The couple had a child and then later moved to Blacktown. After a number of years living in the heart of Blacktown, the family moved to Westmead, to be closer to Joyce's husband's place of work. 2015-12-01
Judith Eastwell Judith Eastwell was born on 14th September, 1945, and lived in Quakers Hill from age 2. Judith's father was the Postmaster at Quakers Hill post office from about 1950 onwards. Judith married and had six children, and eventually took over as Postmistress at Quakers Hill. 2015-12-01
Margaret Taylor Margaret Taylor was born on 23rd February, 1932, in Bradford, Yorkshire, England. Margaret's family moved to Australia when she was 18, settling first at Kingswood and later at Mt Druitt. After leaving school at age 14, Margaret gained a job at St. Mary's Telephone Exchange. Margaret married in 1957 and settled in St Marys with her husband. 2015-12-01
Marie Sing Marie Sing was born on 10th March, 1930, at Wellington, NSW. She moved with her family to Blacktown at age 15. Marie mentions that her family were not happy about her marrying a man with a Chinese background at first, but eventually accepted it. Both Marie and her husband were very involved with Blacktown Bowling Club. When Marie first took up lawn bowls, she had to play at Riverstone because Blacktown club would not accept female players. 2015-12-01
Marjorie Heath Marjorie Heath was born on 28th January, 1926. Marjorie and her husband owned a large poultry farm at Blacktown, near Prospect Reservoir. 2015-12-01
Marjory Turner Marjory Turner was born on the 25th January, 1929, at Penrith. After leaving school at age 15, Marjory worked at Selfridges, Parramatta, and later at a factory in St Marys. Marjory married in 1948 and lived with her husband's family until the couple built a house of their own at Kingswood, Not long after they finished the house, the land was bought by the Council to build flats and so the house was demolished. Marjory and her husband then purchased another block and built a new house in Kingswood. 2015-12-01
Mary Pike Mary Pike was born on 13th December, 1924, at North Sydney and moved to Windsor when she was six weeks old. In 1936, the family moved to Penrith. At the time of the interview, Mary had lived in her house in Lemongrove Road, Penrith, for 50 years. Mary did not marry or have children. She had her own sewing business for a number of years in Penrith during the 1960s. 2015-12-01
Mavis Lamrock Mavis Lamrock was born on 11th January, 1913, at Wauchope. In May, 1914, the family moved to Emu Plains, to a property called 'Yodalla'. Mavis' father planted a lemon orchard and supplied lemons to the lemonade drink company, Tooths. When Mavis married, her father subdivided the block of land along the Nepean River. Mavis and her husband built another home there, called 'Yonder'. 2015-12-01
Olga Robshaw Olga Robshaw was born on the 8th November, 1924, at Riverstone. Her childhood was spent on a small poultry farm at Schofields, which her father had established after retiring from Riverstone Meatworks. Olga married at age 18 and ran 'The Advocate' with her husband. After the marriage broke up Olga took over 'The Advocate' and ran it until 1976. Olga remarried in 1963 and would spend weekends at their property in Bilpin, establishing an apple orchard and enjoying the bushland. 2015-12-01
Olive Price Olive Price was born on 8th February, 1918, at Hornsby. The family moved to Penrith when Olive was aged around 7 or 8. After leaving school Olive attended business college at Parramatta and worked in Sydney and locally. 2015-12-01
Patricia Colless Patricia Colless was born on 1st April, 1922, at Croydon in Sydney. At age 11, Pat moved to Penrith with her family. After getting married at age 23, Pat had two children and lived firstly in Lethbridge Street Penrith, and then at Lapstone. 2015-12-01
Patricia Parker Patricia Parker was born on 19th August, 1937, at the Paddington Women's Hospital in Sydney. Patricia moved to Blacktown with her husband and three children in the mid-1950s at age 21. Both Patricia and her husband joined the Communist Party, and Patricia was a member of the Union of Australian Women for many years. An interest in Community Arts led her to do a degree in Arts Administration, and she was the first Community Arts Officer to be appointed to Blacktown Council. During the interview, Pat mentions Blacktown Council's involvement in the Section 94 court case which resulted in developers having to contribute towards community facilities and infrastructure. 2015-12-01

Photo of Doreen Scott's home Dark brick 2nd storey is home of Doreen Scott on corner of High Street and W--- Street Penrith. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University

Photo of Winsome Phillis Photo of Winsome Phillis. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Rita Camilleri Rita Camilleri was born in Malta and arrived in Australia in 1939, at age 15 years. Prior to that, her father had been in Australia for twelve years, working to save enough money to bring his family to join him. Rita's father owned a market garden in Pendle Hill, and when Rita married at age 18, she and her husband operated another market garden nearby. During the Second World War, Rita worked at the Bonds Cotton Mills, earning five pounds per week in the card room. 2015-12-01
Sheila Nottley Sheila Nottley was born on 12th June, 1908, on a farming property near Kempsey. When Sheila was three months old, her mother died. Sheila moved to Sydney in 1945, firstly to Eastern Creek where she and her husband ran a dairy. They moved to Mt Druitt a couple of years later and rented a cattle property. 2015-12-01
Thelma Masters Thelma Masters was born on 28th June, 1927. When Thelma was aged eight, her mother died. For a while, Thelma, her brothers and her father moved in with her grandmother at Parklea, but then moved back to their original home. This left a large amount of household work and cooking to 10 year old Thelma. After leaving school at 14 and working for a time at Bonds, Thelma met her husband at the Quakers Hill Country Women's Ball and they settled in Quakers Hill. 2015-12-01
Transcript of interview with Alice Potter Alice Potter was born on 30th September, 1929, at Blacktown. Alice's parents owned a house on land which was later bought by Blacktown Workers Club. Alice married in 1954 and the couple lived with her parents for one year, before moving to Rooty Hill. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Amelia Vincent Amelia Vincent was born in 1914, in Italy. Amelia's family migrated to Sydney in 1922, and moved to a new block of land in Blacktown a year later. The family ran a vineyard and Amelia's father worked as a bricklayer. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Amy Jackson Amy Jackson was born on 18th July, 1916, at Penrith and lived her whole life in the Penrith and Emu Plains area. She married in 1943 and later moved to Cambridge Park. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Audrey Watson Audrey Watson was born on 23rd August, 1922 at Sydney. Audrey moved to Penrith with her husband in 1946. The couple lived in a flat above a shop for a number of years before building a house at Emu Plains. Audrey ran the tea room at the Lewer's Bequest Regional Gallery. Like most of the interviewees she was a member of volunteer organisations for many years. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Betty Hargreaves Betty Hargreaves was born on 2nd January, 1918, and moved to Penrith with her family in 1936. Betty met her husband whilst working as a secretary for her father, a produce merchant. Betty started the Penrith Community Aid organisation, as well as a Women's refuge, and was subsequently awarded an Order of Australia for services to the community. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Brenda Niccol Brenda Niccol was born on 20th April, 1920, at the home of her grandmother in Ballarat. She grew up in the Riverina area, on a block of land settled by her father after the first World War. When Brenda was 17, her mother died suddenly. The following year her father sent her to stay at a guest house in Emu Plains, and she later returned there to work as a waitress. Her husband's family owned the guest house and in 1945 Brenda moved there to raise her family and run the guest house with her husband. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Clare Pfoeffer Claire Pfoeffer was born on 30th January, 1913, at Parramatta. Claire moved to Seven Hills when she was aged 10. After leaving school, Claire held a variety of secretarial jobs through her life, working for 28 years at a company named Australian Cream of Tartar as secretary to the Assistant Managing Director. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Diana Reynolds Diana Reynolds was born on 24th January, 1929, at Penrith. Diana's father was one of two doctors in Penrith and the surgery was attached to the house on the corner of High and Evans Street. Diana attended the Australian College of Physical Education after leaving school and then taught for 6 years at Methodist Ladies' College at Burwood. After her sister married, Diana returned home to work for her father as a secretary. Dianna married in the early 1960s and lived in South Penrith with her husband, who was a bomb disposal technician in the Air Force. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Doreen Scott Doreen Scott was born on 1st December, 1925, at Camden. Doreen grew up on a property at Luddenham, moving to Penrith when she married in 1944. Doreen ran Scott's Corner Shop in High Street, Penrith for many years; her husband Jim ran his auto workshop / service station on the same block and the family lived there as well. The family also owned and ran Penrith's Dungowan Theatre, later changing the name to the Avon Theatre. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Edith Mason Edith Mason was born on the 7th August, 1920, at Dubbo. The family moved to Mt Druitt in April, 1927, after her father built a house for them there. Her father worked for the railway. Edith married in 1941 and the couple lived for a short time in Orange, before returning to live with and care for her husband's mother, who developed glaucoma. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Edna Vidler Edna Vidler was born on 31st December, 1908, at Newtown, Sydney. Edna was one of ten children and the eldest girl in her family. The family moved to Blacktown when Edna was aged 12. Edna left school at age 15 and went to work as a tailor in Parramatta. She married in 1930, and sadly her husband was killed in an accident when she was pregnant with their fourth child. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Eugenie Stapleton Eugenie Stapleton was born on 21st January, 1911, at Lambton, Newcastle. Eugenie moved to St. Marys with her husband after the Second World War ended. They bought a new house and settled there with their two children. Eugenie's husband, Len, was the first engineer employed by St Marys Council and was responsible for most of the subdivision of St Marys. The couple also formed the St Marys Historical Society and Eugenie authored a book titled "South Creek, St. Marys - From Village to City" which was published by the Council. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Florence Gibbons Florence Gibbons was born on 11th September, 1908, at Regentville. Florence lived in Penrith for much of her life. After she married in 1935, she and her husband built a house two doors along from Florence's parents in Henry Street, Penrith. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Heather Corr Heather Corr was born on 10th April, 1923, at Penrith. Heather's father was a bootmaker and musician. The family lived in Penrith, in the street now known as Cox Avenue, on a block of land owned by her mother's father. After leaving school at age 14, Heather attended Comptometer college and then worked in Martin Place, Sydney, until she married in 1948. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Iris Hanna Iris Hanna was born on 30th November, 1914, at Lismore. Her father ran a dairy farm at Lismore. Iris married in 1944 and moved to Plumpton at that time, to live on a large poultry farm with her new husband. Iris and her husband had seven children together. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Joan Jeffery Joan Jeffrey was born on 21st June, 1926, at Mudgee. At age 8 she moved with her family to Blacktown, where her father worked as one of two local policemen. The family lived in the police station until the building was demolished to make way for Westpoint Blacktown Shopping Centre. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Joy Willis Joy Willis was born on 5th February, 1926 at St. Marys. Joy attended school in St Marys and Penrith. After leaving school Joy worked for Manpower, who would place her at various locations doing 'war work', until the end of World War 2 in 1945. Joy married in 1947 and the couple built a new home on her father's land, next door to where Joy had grown up. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Joyce McKelvey Joyce McKelvey was born on 16th August, 1918, at Blacktown. Joyce's father was a baker and the family lived in the main street of Blacktown with the bakery in the back of the residence. Joyce recalls seeing the cattle herded through the main street of Blacktown each week, as they were transported from the Flemington markets to be slaughtered at Riverstone Meatworks 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Joyce Moon Joyce Moon was born on 15th March, 1921, at Beelbangera, near Griffith in NSW. Her father worked on Burrinjuck Dam before buying a poultry farm at Schofields, when Joyce was aged 2. Joyce married at age 22 and moved to Riverstone with her husband. The couple had a child and then later moved to Blacktown. After a number of years living in the heart of Blacktown, the family moved to Westmead, to be closer to Joyce's husband's place of work. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Judith Eastwell Judith Eastwell was born on 14th September, 1945, and lived in Quakers Hill from age 2. Judith's father was the Postmaster at Quakers Hill post office from about 1950 onwards. Judith married and had six children, and eventually took over as Postmistress at Quakers Hill. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Margaret Taylor Margaret Taylor was born on 23rd February, 1932, in Bradford, Yorkshire, England. Margaret's family moved to Australia when she was 18, settling first at Kingswood and later at Mt Druitt. After leaving school at age 14, Margaret gained a job at St. Mary's Telephone Exchange. Margaret married in 1957 and settled in St Marys with her husband. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Marie Sing Marie Sing was born on 10th March, 1930, at Wellington, NSW. She moved with her family to Blacktown at age 15. Marie mentions that her family were not happy about her marrying a man with a Chinese background at first, but eventually accepted it. Both Marie and her husband were very involved with Blacktown Bowling Club. When Marie first took up lawn bowls, she had to play at Riverstone because Blacktown club would not accept female players. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Marjorie Heath Marjorie Heath was born on 28th January, 1926. Marjorie and her husband owned a large poultry farm at Blacktown, near Prospect Reservoir. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Marjory Turner Marjory Turner was born on the 25th January, 1929, at Penrith. After leaving school at age 15, Marjory worked at Selfridges, Parramatta, and later at a factory in St Marys. Marjory married in 1948 and lived with her husband's family until the couple built a house of their own at Kingswood, Not long after they finished the house, the land was bought by the Council to build flats and so the house was demolished. Marjory and her husband then purchased another block and built a new house in Kingswood. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Mary Pike Mary Pike was born on 13th December, 1924, at North Sydney and moved to Windsor when she was six weeks old. In 1936, the family moved to Penrith. At the time of the interview, Mary had lived in her house in Lemongrove Road, Penrith, for 50 years. Mary did not marry or have children. She had her own sewing business for a number of years in Penrith during the 1960s. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Mavis Lamrock Mavis Lamrock was born on 11th January, 1913, at Wauchope. In May, 1914, the family moved to Emu Plains, to a property called 'Yodalla'. Mavis' father planted a lemon orchard and supplied lemons to the lemonade drink company, Tooths. When Mavis married, her father subdivided the block of land along the Nepean River. Mavis and her husband built another home there, called 'Yonder'. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Olga Robshaw Olga Robshaw was born on the 8th November, 1924, at Riverstone. Her childhood was spent on a small poultry farm at Schofields, which her father had established after retiring from Riverstone Meatworks. Olga married at age 18 and ran 'The Advocate' with her husband. After the marriage broke up Olga took over 'The Advocate' and ran it until 1976. Olga remarried in 1963 and would spend weekends at their property in Bilpin, establishing an apple orchard and enjoying the bushland. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Olive Price Olive Price was born on 8th February, 1918, at Hornsby. The family moved to Penrith when Olive was aged around 7 or 8. After leaving school Olive attended business college at Parramatta and worked in Sydney and locally. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Patricia Colless Patricia Colless was born on 1st April, 1922, at Croydon in Sydney. At age 11, Pat moved to Penrith with her family. After getting married at age 23, Pat had two children and lived firstly in Lethbridge Street Penrith, and then at Lapstone. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Patricia Parker Patricia Parker was born on 19th August, 1937, at the Paddington Women's Hospital in Sydney. Patricia moved to Blacktown with her husband and three children in the mid-1950s at age 21. Both Patricia and her husband joined the Communist Party, and Patricia was a member of the Union of Australian Women for many years. An interest in Community Arts led her to do a degree in Arts Administration, and she was the first Community Arts Officer to be appointed to Blacktown Council. During the interview, Pat mentions Blacktown Council's involvement in the Section 94 court case which resulted in developers having to contribute towards community facilities and infrastructure. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Rita Camilleri Rita Camilleri was born in Malta and arrived in Australia in 1939, at age 15 years. Prior to that, her father had been in Australia for twelve years, working to save enough money to bring his family to join him. Rita's father owned a market garden in Pendle Hill, and when Rita married at age 18, she and her husband operated another market garden nearby. During the Second World War, Rita worked at the Bonds Cotton Mills, earning five pounds per week in the card room. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Sheila Nottley Sheila Nottley was born on 12th June, 1908, on a farming property near Kempsey. When Sheila was three months old, her mother died. Sheila moved to Sydney in 1945, firstly to Eastern Creek where she and her husband ran a dairy. They moved to Mt Druitt a couple of years later and rented a cattle property. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Thelma Masters Thelma Masters was born on 28th June, 1927. When Thelma was aged eight, her mother died. For a while, Thelma, her brothers and her father moved in with her grandmother at Parklea, but then moved back to their original home. This left a large amount of household work and cooking to 10 year old Thelma. After leaving school at 14 and working for a time at Bonds, Thelma met her husband at the Quakers Hill Country Women's Ball and they settled in Quakers Hill. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Transcript of interview with Winsome Phillis Winsome Phillis was born on 5th March, 1929, at Riverstone. Winsome's father worked at the Riverstone Meatworks and the family lived in a house they had built in Regent Street, Riverstone. After leaving school at age 15, Phillis completed a Comptometer course and later worked at the Riverstone Meatworks using an accounting machine. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Flier Flier seeking participants for the project. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Flier (illustrated) Flier (illustrated) seeking participants for the project. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University

Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Letter from Eugenie Stapleton Letter thanking the interviewer, accompanied by a list of corrections to be made to the transcript. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Eugenie Stapleton Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Letter from Mavis Lamrock Letter written on "Yonder" stationery (3 pages). 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Mavis Lamrock Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Letter from Winsome Phillis Letter written after the interview, highlighting a few corrections needed to the transcript and providing additional detail around some points. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Winsome Phillis Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Second letter from Mavis Lamrock Letter written on "Yonder" stationery (1 page). 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Mavis Lamrock Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Sources used for finding informants This document lists organisations and other sources, through which suitable participants were sought for interviews. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Thank-you note from Olive Price A short thank-you note, returned to the interviewer with a corrected version of the transcript. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Olive Price Western Sydney University
Winsome Phillis Winsome Phillis was born on 5th March, 1929, at Riverstone. Winsome's father worked at the Riverstone Meatworks and the family lived in a house they had built in Regent Street, Riverstone. After leaving school at age 15, Phillis completed a Comptometer course and later worked at the Riverstone Meatworks using an accounting machine. 2015-12-01

name familyName givenName description datePublished pcdm:memberOf
Alice Potter Alice Potter was born on 30th September, 1929, at Blacktown. Alice's parents owned a house on land which was later bought by Blacktown Workers Club. Alice married in 1954 and the couple lived with her parents for one year, before moving to Rooty Hill. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Amelia Vincent Amelia Vincent was born in 1914, in Italy. Amelia's family migrated to Sydney in 1922, and moved to a new block of land in Blacktown a year later. The family ran a vineyard and Amelia's father worked as a bricklayer. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Amy Jackson Amy Jackson was born on 18th July, 1916, at Penrith and lived her whole life in the Penrith and Emu Plains area. She married in 1943 and later moved to Cambridge Park. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Audrey Watson Audrey Watson was born on 23rd August, 1922 at Sydney. Audrey moved to Penrith with her husband in 1946. The couple lived in a flat above a shop for a number of years before building a house at Emu Plains. Audrey ran the tea room at the Lewer's Bequest regional gallery and like most of the interviewees she was a member of volunteer organisations for many years. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Betty Hargreaves Betty Hargreaves was born on 2nd January, 1918, and moved to Penrith with her family in 1936. Betty met her husband whilst working as a secretary for her father, a produce merchant. Betty started the Penrith Community Aid organisation, as well as a Women's refuge, and was subsequently awarded an Order of Australia for services to the community. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Brenda Niccol Brenda Niccol was born on 20th April, 1920, at the home of her grandmother in Ballarat. She grew up in the Riverina area, on a block of land settled by her father after the first World War. When Brenda was 17, her mother died suddenly. The following year her father sent her to stay at a guest house in Emu Plains, and she later returned there to work as a waitress. Her husband's family owned the guest house and in 1945 Brenda moved there to raise her family and run the guest house with her husband. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Carol Liston Liston Carol
Christine Wieneke Wieneke Christine
Clare Pfoeffer Claire Pfoeffer was born on 30th January, 1913, at Parramatta. Claire moved to Seven Hills when she was aged 10. After leaving school, Claire held a variety of secretarial jobs through her life, working for 28 years at a company named Australian Cream of Tartar as secretary to the Assistant Managing Director. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Contact Katrina Trewin
Deborah Chambers Chambers Deborah
Diana Reynolds Diana Reynolds was born on 24th January, 1929, at Penrith. Diana's father was one of two doctors in Penrith and the surgery was attached to the house on the corner of High and Evans Street. Diana attended the Australian College of Physical Education after leaving school and then taught for 6 years at Methodist Ladies' College at Burwood. After her sister married, Diana returned home to work for her father as a secretary. Dianna married in the early 1960s and lived in South Penrith with her husband, who was a bomb disposal technician in the Air Force. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Doreen Scott Doreen Scott was born on 1st December, 1925, at Camden. Doreen grew up on a property at Luddenham, moving to Penrith when she married in 1944. Doreen ran Scott's Corner Shop in High Street, Penrith for many years; her husband Jim ran his auto workshop / service station on the same block and the family lived there as well. The family also owned and ran Penrith's Dungowan Theatre, later changing the name to the Avon Theatre. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Edith Mason Edith Mason was born on the 7th August, 1920, at Dubbo. The family moved to Mt Druitt in April, 1927, after her father built a house for them there. Her father worked for the railway. Edith married in 1941 and the couple lived for a short time in Orange, before returning to live with and care for her husband's mother, who developed glaucoma. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Edna Vidler Edna Vidler was born on 31st December, 1908, at Newtown, Sydney. Edna was one of ten children and the eldest girl in her family. The family moved to Blacktown when Edna was aged 12. Edna left school at age 15 and went to work as a tailor in Parramatta. She married in 1930, and sadly her husband was killed in an accident when she was pregnant with their fourth child. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Eugenie Stapleton Eugenie Stapleton was born on 21st January, 1911, at Lambton, Newcastle. Eugenie moved to St. Marys with her husband after the Second World War ended. They bought a new house and settled there with their two children. Eugenie's husband, Len, was the first engineer employed by St Marys Council and was responsible for most of the subdivision of St Marys. The couple also formed the St Marys Historical Society and Eugenie authored a book titled "South Creek, St. Marys - From Village to City" which was published by the Council. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Eugenie Stapleton Stapleton Eugenie
Florence Gibbons Florence Gibbons was born on 11th September, 1908, at Regentville. Florence lived in Penrith for much of her life. After she married in 1935, she and her husband built a house two doors along from Florence's parents in Henry Street, Penrith. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Heather Corr Heather Corr was born on 10th April, 1923, at Penrith. Heather's father was a bootmaker and musician. The family lived in Penrith, in the street now known as Cox Avenue, on a block of land owned by her mother's father. After leaving school at age 14, Heather attended comptometer college and then worked in Martin Place, Sydney, until she married in 1948. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Iris Hanna Iris Hanna was born on 30th November, 1914, at Lismore. Her father ran a dairy farm at Lismore. Iris married in 1944 and moved to Plumpton at that time, to live on a large poultry farm with her new husband. Iris and her husband had seven children together. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Joan Jeffery Joan Jeffrey was born on 21st June, 1926, at Mudgee. At age 8 she moved with her family to Blacktown, where her father worked as one of two local policemen. The family lived in the police station until the building was demolished to make way for Westpoint Blacktown Shopping Centre. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Joy Willis Joy Willis was born on 5th February, 1926 at St. Marys. Joy attended school in St Marys and Penrith. After leaving school Joy worked for Manpower, who would place her at various locations doing 'war work', until the end of World War 2 in 1945. Joy married in 1947 and the couple built a new home on her father's land, next door to where Joy had grown up. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Joyce McKelvey Joyce McKelvey was born on 16th August, 1918, at Blacktown. Joyce's father was a baker and the family lived in the main street of Blacktown with the bakery in the back of the residence. Joyce recalls seeing the cattle herded through the main street of Blacktown each week, as they were transported from the Flemington markets to be slaughtered at Riverstone Meatworks 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Joyce Moon Joyce Moon was born on 15th March, 1921, at Beelbangera, near Griffith in NSW. Her father worked on Burrinjuck Dam before buying a poultry farm at Schofields, when Joyce was aged 2. Joyce married at age 22 and moved to Riverstone with her husband. The couple had a child and then later moved to Blacktown. After a number of years living in the heart of Blacktown, the family moved to Westmead, to be closer to Joyce's husband's place of work. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Judith Eastwell Judith Eastwell was born on 14th September, 1945, and lived in Quakers Hill from age 2. Judith's father was the Postmaster at Quakers Hill post office from about 1950 onwards. Judith married and had six children, and eventually took over as Postmistress at Quakers Hill. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Margaret Taylor Margaret Taylor was born on 23rd February, 1932, in Bradford, Yorkshire, England. Margaret's family moved to Australia when she was 18, settling first at Kingswood and later at Mt Druitt. After leaving school at age 14, Margaret gained a job at St. Mary's Telephone Exchange. Margaret married in 1957 and settled in St Marys with her husband. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Marie Sing Marie Sing was born on 10th March, 1930, at Wellington, NSW. She moved with her family to Blacktown at age 15. Marie mentions that her family were not happy about her marrying a man with a Chinese background at first, but eventually accepted it. Both Marie and her husband were very involved with Blacktown Bowling Club. When Marie first took up lawn bowls, she had to play at Riverstone because Blacktown club would not accept female players. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Marjorie Heath Marjorie Heath was born on 28th January, 1926. Marjorie and her husband owned a large poultry farm at Blacktown, near Prospect Reservoir. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Marjory Turner Marjory Turner was born on the 25th January, 1929, at Penrith. After leaving school at age 15, Marjory worked at Selfridges, Parramatta, and later at a factory in St Marys. Marjory married in 1948 and lived with her husband's family until the couple built a house of their own at Kingswood, Not long after they finished the house, the land was bought by the Council to build flats and so the house was demolished. Marjory and her husband then purchased another block and built a new house in Kingswood. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Mary Pike Mary Pike was born on 13th December, 1924, at North Sydney and moved to Windsor when she was six weeks old. In 1936, the family moved to Penrith. At the time of the interview, Mary had lived in her house in Lemongrove Road, Penrith, for 50 years. Mary did not marry or have children. She had her own sewing business for a number of years in Penrith during the 1960s. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Mavis Lamrock Mavis Lamrock was born on 11th January, 1913, at Wauchope. In May, 1914, the family moved to Emu Plains, to a property called 'Yodalla'. Mavis' father planted a lemon orchard and supplied lemons to the lemonade drink company, Tooths. When Mavis married, her father subdivided the block of land along the Nepean River. Mavis and her husband built another home there, called 'Yonder'. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Mavis Lamrock Lamrock Mavis
Olga Robshaw Olga Robshaw was born on the 8th November, 1924, at Riverstone. Her childhood was spent on a small poultry farm at Schofields, which her father had established after retiring from Riverstone Meatworks. Olga married at age 18 and ran 'The Advocate' with her husband. After the marriage broke up Olga took over 'The Advocate' and ran it until 1976. Olga remarried in 1963 and would spend weekends at their property in Bilpin, establishing an apple orchard and enjoying the bushland. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Olive Price Olive Price was born on 8th February, 1918, at Hornsby. The family moved to Penrith when Olive was aged around 7 or 8. After leaving school Olive attended business college at Parramatta and worked in Sydney and locally. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Olive Price Price Olive
Patricia Colless Patricia Colless was born on 1st April, 1922, at Croydon in Sydney. At age 11, Pat moved to Penrith with her family. After getting married at age 23, Pat had two children and lived firstly in Lethbridge Street Penrith, and then at Lapstone. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Patricia Parker Patricia Parker was born on 19th August, 1937, at the Paddington Women's Hospital in Sydney. Patricia moved to Blacktown with her husband and three children in the mid-1950s at age 21. Both Patricia and her husband joined the Communist Party, and Patricia was a member of the Union of Australian Women for many years. An interest in Community Arts led her to do a degree in Arts Administration, and she was the first Community Arts Officer to be appointed to Blacktown Council. During the interview, Pat mentions Blacktown Council's involvement in the Section 94 court case which resulted in developers having to contribute towards community facilities and infrastructure. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Rita Camilleri Rita Camilleri was born in Malta and arrived in Australia in 1939, at age 15 years. Prior to that, her father had been in Australia for twelve years, working to save enough money to bring his family to join him. Rita's father owned a market garden in Pendle Hill, and when Rita married at age 18, she and her husband operated another market garden nearby. During the Second World War, Rita worked at the Bonds Cotton Mills, earning five pounds per week in the card room. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Sheila Nottley Sheila Nottley was born on 12th June, 1908, on a farming property near Kempsey. When Sheila was three months old, her mother died. Sheila moved to Sydney in 1945, firstly to Eastern Creek where she and her husband ran a dairy. They moved to Mt Druitt a couple of years later and rented a cattle property. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Thelma Masters Thelma Masters was born on 28th June, 1927. When Thelma was aged eight, her mother died. For a while, Thelma, her brothers and her father moved in with her grandmother at Parklea, but then moved back to their original home. This left a large amount of household work and cooking to 10 year old Thelma. After leaving school at 14 and working for a time at Bonds, Thelma met her husband at the Quakers Hill Country Women's Ball and they settled in Quakers Hill. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Winsome Phillis Winsome Phillis was born on 5th March, 1929, at Riverstone. Winsome's father worked at the Riverstone Meatworks and the family lived in a house they had built in Regent Street, Riverstone. After leaving school at age 15, Phillis completed a Comptometer course and later worked at the Riverstone Meatworks using an accounting machine. 2015-12-01 Interviewees
Winsome Phillis Phillis Winsome

name description datePublished license publisher pcdm:memberOf
Transcript of interview with Alice Potter Alice Potter was born on 30th September, 1929, at Blacktown. Alice's parents owned a house on land which was later bought by Blacktown Workers Club. Alice married in 1954 and the couple lived with her parents for one year, before moving to Rooty Hill. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Amelia Vincent Amelia Vincent was born in 1914, in Italy. Amelia's family migrated to Sydney in 1922, and moved to a new block of land in Blacktown a year later. The family ran a vineyard and Amelia's father worked as a bricklayer. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Amy Jackson Amy Jackson was born on 18th July, 1916, at Penrith and lived her whole life in the Penrith and Emu Plains area. She married in 1943 and later moved to Cambridge Park. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Audrey Watson Audrey Watson was born on 23rd August, 1922 at Sydney. Audrey moved to Penrith with her husband in 1946. The couple lived in a flat above a shop for a number of years before building a house at Emu Plains. Audrey ran the tea room at the Lewer's Bequest Regional Gallery. Like most of the interviewees she was a member of volunteer organisations for many years. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Betty Hargreaves Betty Hargreaves was born on 2nd January, 1918, and moved to Penrith with her family in 1936. Betty met her husband whilst working as a secretary for her father, a produce merchant. Betty started the Penrith Community Aid organisation, as well as a Women's refuge, and was subsequently awarded an Order of Australia for services to the community. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Brenda Niccol Brenda Niccol was born on 20th April, 1920, at the home of her grandmother in Ballarat. She grew up in the Riverina area, on a block of land settled by her father after the first World War. When Brenda was 17, her mother died suddenly. The following year her father sent her to stay at a guest house in Emu Plains, and she later returned there to work as a waitress. Her husband's family owned the guest house and in 1945 Brenda moved there to raise her family and run the guest house with her husband. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Clare Pfoeffer Claire Pfoeffer was born on 30th January, 1913, at Parramatta. Claire moved to Seven Hills when she was aged 10. After leaving school, Claire held a variety of secretarial jobs through her life, working for 28 years at a company named Australian Cream of Tartar as secretary to the Assistant Managing Director. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Diana Reynolds Diana Reynolds was born on 24th January, 1929, at Penrith. Diana's father was one of two doctors in Penrith and the surgery was attached to the house on the corner of High and Evans Street. Diana attended the Australian College of Physical Education after leaving school and then taught for 6 years at Methodist Ladies' College at Burwood. After her sister married, Diana returned home to work for her father as a secretary. Dianna married in the early 1960s and lived in South Penrith with her husband, who was a bomb disposal technician in the Air Force. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Doreen Scott Doreen Scott was born on 1st December, 1925, at Camden. Doreen grew up on a property at Luddenham, moving to Penrith when she married in 1944. Doreen ran Scott's Corner Shop in High Street, Penrith for many years; her husband Jim ran his auto workshop / service station on the same block and the family lived there as well. The family also owned and ran Penrith's Dungowan Theatre, later changing the name to the Avon Theatre. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Edith Mason Edith Mason was born on the 7th August, 1920, at Dubbo. The family moved to Mt Druitt in April, 1927, after her father built a house for them there. Her father worked for the railway. Edith married in 1941 and the couple lived for a short time in Orange, before returning to live with and care for her husband's mother, who developed glaucoma. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Edna Vidler Edna Vidler was born on 31st December, 1908, at Newtown, Sydney. Edna was one of ten children and the eldest girl in her family. The family moved to Blacktown when Edna was aged 12. Edna left school at age 15 and went to work as a tailor in Parramatta. She married in 1930, and sadly her husband was killed in an accident when she was pregnant with their fourth child. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Eugenie Stapleton Eugenie Stapleton was born on 21st January, 1911, at Lambton, Newcastle. Eugenie moved to St. Marys with her husband after the Second World War ended. They bought a new house and settled there with their two children. Eugenie's husband, Len, was the first engineer employed by St Marys Council and was responsible for most of the subdivision of St Marys. The couple also formed the St Marys Historical Society and Eugenie authored a book titled "South Creek, St. Marys - From Village to City" which was published by the Council. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Florence Gibbons Florence Gibbons was born on 11th September, 1908, at Regentville. Florence lived in Penrith for much of her life. After she married in 1935, she and her husband built a house two doors along from Florence's parents in Henry Street, Penrith. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Heather Corr Heather Corr was born on 10th April, 1923, at Penrith. Heather's father was a bootmaker and musician. The family lived in Penrith, in the street now known as Cox Avenue, on a block of land owned by her mother's father. After leaving school at age 14, Heather attended Comptometer college and then worked in Martin Place, Sydney, until she married in 1948. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Iris Hanna Iris Hanna was born on 30th November, 1914, at Lismore. Her father ran a dairy farm at Lismore. Iris married in 1944 and moved to Plumpton at that time, to live on a large poultry farm with her new husband. Iris and her husband had seven children together. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Joan Jeffery Joan Jeffrey was born on 21st June, 1926, at Mudgee. At age 8 she moved with her family to Blacktown, where her father worked as one of two local policemen. The family lived in the police station until the building was demolished to make way for Westpoint Blacktown Shopping Centre. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Joy Willis Joy Willis was born on 5th February, 1926 at St. Marys. Joy attended school in St Marys and Penrith. After leaving school Joy worked for Manpower, who would place her at various locations doing 'war work', until the end of World War 2 in 1945. Joy married in 1947 and the couple built a new home on her father's land, next door to where Joy had grown up. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Joyce McKelvey Joyce McKelvey was born on 16th August, 1918, at Blacktown. Joyce's father was a baker and the family lived in the main street of Blacktown with the bakery in the back of the residence. Joyce recalls seeing the cattle herded through the main street of Blacktown each week, as they were transported from the Flemington markets to be slaughtered at Riverstone Meatworks 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Joyce Moon Joyce Moon was born on 15th March, 1921, at Beelbangera, near Griffith in NSW. Her father worked on Burrinjuck Dam before buying a poultry farm at Schofields, when Joyce was aged 2. Joyce married at age 22 and moved to Riverstone with her husband. The couple had a child and then later moved to Blacktown. After a number of years living in the heart of Blacktown, the family moved to Westmead, to be closer to Joyce's husband's place of work. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Judith Eastwell Judith Eastwell was born on 14th September, 1945, and lived in Quakers Hill from age 2. Judith's father was the Postmaster at Quakers Hill post office from about 1950 onwards. Judith married and had six children, and eventually took over as Postmistress at Quakers Hill. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Margaret Taylor Margaret Taylor was born on 23rd February, 1932, in Bradford, Yorkshire, England. Margaret's family moved to Australia when she was 18, settling first at Kingswood and later at Mt Druitt. After leaving school at age 14, Margaret gained a job at St. Mary's Telephone Exchange. Margaret married in 1957 and settled in St Marys with her husband. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Marie Sing Marie Sing was born on 10th March, 1930, at Wellington, NSW. She moved with her family to Blacktown at age 15. Marie mentions that her family were not happy about her marrying a man with a Chinese background at first, but eventually accepted it. Both Marie and her husband were very involved with Blacktown Bowling Club. When Marie first took up lawn bowls, she had to play at Riverstone because Blacktown club would not accept female players. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Marjorie Heath Marjorie Heath was born on 28th January, 1926. Marjorie and her husband owned a large poultry farm at Blacktown, near Prospect Reservoir. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Marjory Turner Marjory Turner was born on the 25th January, 1929, at Penrith. After leaving school at age 15, Marjory worked at Selfridges, Parramatta, and later at a factory in St Marys. Marjory married in 1948 and lived with her husband's family until the couple built a house of their own at Kingswood, Not long after they finished the house, the land was bought by the Council to build flats and so the house was demolished. Marjory and her husband then purchased another block and built a new house in Kingswood. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Mary Pike Mary Pike was born on 13th December, 1924, at North Sydney and moved to Windsor when she was six weeks old. In 1936, the family moved to Penrith. At the time of the interview, Mary had lived in her house in Lemongrove Road, Penrith, for 50 years. Mary did not marry or have children. She had her own sewing business for a number of years in Penrith during the 1960s. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Mavis Lamrock Mavis Lamrock was born on 11th January, 1913, at Wauchope. In May, 1914, the family moved to Emu Plains, to a property called 'Yodalla'. Mavis' father planted a lemon orchard and supplied lemons to the lemonade drink company, Tooths. When Mavis married, her father subdivided the block of land along the Nepean River. Mavis and her husband built another home there, called 'Yonder'. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Olga Robshaw Olga Robshaw was born on the 8th November, 1924, at Riverstone. Her childhood was spent on a small poultry farm at Schofields, which her father had established after retiring from Riverstone Meatworks. Olga married at age 18 and ran 'The Advocate' with her husband. After the marriage broke up Olga took over 'The Advocate' and ran it until 1976. Olga remarried in 1963 and would spend weekends at their property in Bilpin, establishing an apple orchard and enjoying the bushland. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Olive Price Olive Price was born on 8th February, 1918, at Hornsby. The family moved to Penrith when Olive was aged around 7 or 8. After leaving school Olive attended business college at Parramatta and worked in Sydney and locally. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Patricia Colless Patricia Colless was born on 1st April, 1922, at Croydon in Sydney. At age 11, Pat moved to Penrith with her family. After getting married at age 23, Pat had two children and lived firstly in Lethbridge Street Penrith, and then at Lapstone. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Patricia Parker Patricia Parker was born on 19th August, 1937, at the Paddington Women's Hospital in Sydney. Patricia moved to Blacktown with her husband and three children in the mid-1950s at age 21. Both Patricia and her husband joined the Communist Party, and Patricia was a member of the Union of Australian Women for many years. An interest in Community Arts led her to do a degree in Arts Administration, and she was the first Community Arts Officer to be appointed to Blacktown Council. During the interview, Pat mentions Blacktown Council's involvement in the Section 94 court case which resulted in developers having to contribute towards community facilities and infrastructure. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Rita Camilleri Rita Camilleri was born in Malta and arrived in Australia in 1939, at age 15 years. Prior to that, her father had been in Australia for twelve years, working to save enough money to bring his family to join him. Rita's father owned a market garden in Pendle Hill, and when Rita married at age 18, she and her husband operated another market garden nearby. During the Second World War, Rita worked at the Bonds Cotton Mills, earning five pounds per week in the card room. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Sheila Nottley Sheila Nottley was born on 12th June, 1908, on a farming property near Kempsey. When Sheila was three months old, her mother died. Sheila moved to Sydney in 1945, firstly to Eastern Creek where she and her husband ran a dairy. They moved to Mt Druitt a couple of years later and rented a cattle property. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Thelma Masters Thelma Masters was born on 28th June, 1927. When Thelma was aged eight, her mother died. For a while, Thelma, her brothers and her father moved in with her grandmother at Parklea, but then moved back to their original home. This left a large amount of household work and cooking to 10 year old Thelma. After leaving school at 14 and working for a time at Bonds, Thelma met her husband at the Quakers Hill Country Women's Ball and they settled in Quakers Hill. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts
Transcript of interview with Winsome Phillis Winsome Phillis was born on 5th March, 1929, at Riverstone. Winsome's father worked at the Riverstone Meatworks and the family lived in a house they had built in Regent Street, Riverstone. After leaving school at age 15, Phillis completed a Comptometer course and later worked at the Riverstone Meatworks using an accounting machine. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Interview Transcripts

name description datePublished license creator publisher
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Flier Flier seeking participants for the project. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Flier (illustrated) Flier (illustrated) seeking participants for the project. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Interview question outline Outline of questions for conducting oral history interviews. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Letter from Eugenie Stapleton Letter thanking the interviewer, accompanied by a list of corrections to be made to the transcript. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Eugenie Stapleton Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Letter from Mavis Lamrock Letter written on "Yonder" stationery (3 pages). 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Mavis Lamrock Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Letter from Winsome Phillis Letter written after the interview, highlighting a few corrections needed to the transcript and providing additional detail around some points. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Winsome Phillis Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Second letter from Mavis Lamrock Letter written on "Yonder" stationery (1 page). 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Mavis Lamrock Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Sources used for finding informants This document lists organisations and other sources, through which suitable participants were sought for interviews. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University
Western Sydney Women's Oral History Project: Thank-you note from Olive Price A short thank-you note, returned to the interviewer with a corrected version of the transcript. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Olive Price Western Sydney University
name description datePublished license publisher accountablePerson
Interview Audio Recordings 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Contact Katrina Trewin
Interview Transcripts Transcripts of the recorded interviews with each participant. Some transcripts were available as existing Microsoft Word documents, others were scanned to PDF format and converted to Microsoft Word using OCR software. All transcripts were then converted to PDF/A format for inclusion in this site. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Contact Katrina Trewin
Interviewees Information about the people interviewed for the Farms to Freeways collection 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Contact Katrina Trewin
Letters and Notes Letters to Robyn Arrowsmith, written by participants after the interview. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Contact Katrina Trewin
Photographs Photographs taken at the time of the interview with each participant. These mainly include images of the interviewee at her home; there are also some images of the surrounding area to the interview location. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Contact Katrina Trewin
Project Materials Materials associated with the project, including fliers seeking participants, lists of sources and question outline. 2015-12-01 Attribution 3.0 Australia (CC BY 3.0 AU) Western Sydney University Contact Katrina Trewin

Transcripts of the recorded interviews with each participant. Some transcripts were available as existing Microsoft Word documents, others were scanned to PDF format and converted to Microsoft Word using OCR software. All transcripts were then converted to PDF/A format for inclusion in this site.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Patricia Colless

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/427

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Patricia Colless
description ⓘ
Patricia Colless was born on 1st April, 1922, at Croydon in Sydney. At age 11, Pat moved to Penrith with her family. After getting married at age 23, Pat had two children and lived firstly in Lethbridge Street Penrith, and then at Lapstone.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Rita Camilleri

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/428

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Rita Camilleri
description ⓘ
Rita Camilleri was born in Malta and arrived in Australia in 1939, at age 15 years. Prior to that, her father had been in Australia for twelve years, working to save enough money to bring his family to join him. Rita's father owned a market garden in Pendle Hill, and when Rita married at age 18, she and her husband operated another market garden nearby. During the Second World War, Rita worked at the Bonds Cotton Mills, earning five pounds per week in the card room.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Heather Corr

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/429

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Heather Corr
description ⓘ
Heather Corr was born on 10th April, 1923, at Penrith. Heather's father was a bootmaker and musician. The family lived in Penrith, in the street now known as Cox Avenue, on a block of land owned by her mother's father. After leaving school at age 14, Heather attended Comptometer college and then worked in Martin Place, Sydney, until she married in 1948.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Judith Eastwell

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/430

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Judith Eastwell
description ⓘ
Judith Eastwell was born on 14th September, 1945, and lived in Quakers Hill from age 2. Judith's father was the Postmaster at Quakers Hill post office from about 1950 onwards. Judith married and had six children, and eventually took over as Postmistress at Quakers Hill.

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Florence Gibbons
description ⓘ
Florence Gibbons was born on 11th September, 1908, at Regentville. Florence lived in Penrith for much of her life. After she married in 1935, she and her husband built a house two doors along from Florence's parents in Henry Street, Penrith.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Iris Hanna

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/432

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Iris Hanna
description ⓘ
Iris Hanna was born on 30th November, 1914, at Lismore. Her father ran a dairy farm at Lismore. Iris married in 1944 and moved to Plumpton at that time, to live on a large poultry farm with her new husband. Iris and her husband had seven children together.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Betty Hargreaves

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/433

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Betty Hargreaves
description ⓘ
Betty Hargreaves was born on 2nd January, 1918, and moved to Penrith with her family in 1936. Betty met her husband whilst working as a secretary for her father, a produce merchant. Betty started the Penrith Community Aid organisation, as well as a Women's refuge, and was subsequently awarded an Order of Australia for services to the community.

Transcript of interview with Amy Jackson
description ⓘ
Amy Jackson was born on 18th July, 1916, at Penrith and lived her whole life in the Penrith and Emu Plains area. She married in 1943 and later moved to Cambridge Park.

Transcript of interview with Joan Jeffery

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/436

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Joan Jeffery
description ⓘ
Joan Jeffrey was born on 21st June, 1926, at Mudgee. At age 8 she moved with her family to Blacktown, where her father worked as one of two local policemen. The family lived in the police station until the building was demolished to make way for Westpoint Blacktown Shopping Centre.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Mavis Lamrock

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/437

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Mavis Lamrock
description ⓘ
Mavis Lamrock was born on 11th January, 1913, at Wauchope. In May, 1914, the family moved to Emu Plains, to a property called 'Yodalla'. Mavis' father planted a lemon orchard and supplied lemons to the lemonade drink company, Tooths. When Mavis married, her father subdivided the block of land along the Nepean River. Mavis and her husband built another home there, called 'Yonder'.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Edith Mason

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/438

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Edith Mason
description ⓘ
Edith Mason was born on the 7th August, 1920, at Dubbo. The family moved to Mt Druitt in April, 1927, after her father built a house for them there. Her father worked for the railway. Edith married in 1941 and the couple lived for a short time in Orange, before returning to live with and care for her husband's mother, who developed glaucoma.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Thelma Masters

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/439

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Thelma Masters
description ⓘ
Thelma Masters was born on 28th June, 1927. When Thelma was aged eight, her mother died. For a while, Thelma, her brothers and her father moved in with her grandmother at Parklea, but then moved back to their original home. This left a large amount of household work and cooking to 10 year old Thelma. After leaving school at 14 and working for a time at Bonds, Thelma met her husband at the Quakers Hill Country Women's Ball and they settled in Quakers Hill.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Joyce McKelvey

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/440

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Joyce McKelvey
description ⓘ
Joyce McKelvey was born on 16th August, 1918, at Blacktown. Joyce's father was a baker and the family lived in the main street of Blacktown with the bakery in the back of the residence. Joyce recalls seeing the cattle herded through the main street of Blacktown each week, as they were transported from the Flemington markets to be slaughtered at Riverstone Meatworks

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Joyce Moon

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/441

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Joyce Moon
description ⓘ
Joyce Moon was born on 15th March, 1921, at Beelbangera, near Griffith in NSW. Her father worked on Burrinjuck Dam before buying a poultry farm at Schofields, when Joyce was aged 2. Joyce married at age 22 and moved to Riverstone with her husband. The couple had a child and then later moved to Blacktown. After a number of years living in the heart of Blacktown, the family moved to Westmead, to be closer to Joyce's husband's place of work.

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Brenda Niccol
description ⓘ
Brenda Niccol was born on 20th April, 1920, at the home of her grandmother in Ballarat. She grew up in the Riverina area, on a block of land settled by her father after the first World War. When Brenda was 17, her mother died suddenly. The following year her father sent her to stay at a guest house in Emu Plains, and she later returned there to work as a waitress. Her husband's family owned the guest house and in 1945 Brenda moved there to raise her family and run the guest house with her husband.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Sheila Nottley

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/443

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Sheila Nottley
description ⓘ
Sheila Nottley was born on 12th June, 1908, on a farming property near Kempsey. When Sheila was three months old, her mother died. Sheila moved to Sydney in 1945, firstly to Eastern Creek where she and her husband ran a dairy. They moved to Mt Druitt a couple of years later and rented a cattle property.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Patricia Parker

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/444

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Patricia Parker
description ⓘ
Patricia Parker was born on 19th August, 1937, at the Paddington Women's Hospital in Sydney. Patricia moved to Blacktown with her husband and three children in the mid-1950s at age 21. Both Patricia and her husband joined the Communist Party, and Patricia was a member of the Union of Australian Women for many years. An interest in Community Arts led her to do a degree in Arts Administration, and she was the first Community Arts Officer to be appointed to Blacktown Council. During the interview, Pat mentions Blacktown Council's involvement in the Section 94 court case which resulted in developers having to contribute towards community facilities and infrastructure.

Transcript of interview with Clare Pfoeffer

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/445

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Clare Pfoeffer
description ⓘ
Claire Pfoeffer was born on 30th January, 1913, at Parramatta. Claire moved to Seven Hills when she was aged 10. After leaving school, Claire held a variety of secretarial jobs through her life, working for 28 years at a company named Australian Cream of Tartar as secretary to the Assistant Managing Director.

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Winsome Phillis
description ⓘ
Winsome Phillis was born on 5th March, 1929, at Riverstone. Winsome's father worked at the Riverstone Meatworks and the family lived in a house they had built in Regent Street, Riverstone. After leaving school at age 15, Phillis completed a Comptometer course and later worked at the Riverstone Meatworks using an accounting machine.

Interview Transcript ⓘ

Transcript of interview with Mary Pike

arcp://name,hdl10.5281~zenodo.19123230/farmstofreeways/api/items/447

About
conformsTo ⓘ
https://w3id.org/ldac/profile#Object
name ⓘ
Transcript of interview with Mary Pike
description ⓘ
Mary Pike was born on 13th December, 1924, at North Sydney and moved to Windsor when she was six weeks old. In 1936, the family moved to Penrith. At the time of the interview, Mary had lived in her house in Lemongrove Road, Penrith, for 50 years. Mary did not marry or have children. She had her own sewing business for a number of years in Penrith during the 1960s.

Transcript of interview with Alice Potter
description ⓘ
Alice Potter was born on 30th September, 1929, at Blacktown. Alice's parents owned a house on land which was later bought by Blacktown Workers Club. Alice married in 1954 and the couple lived with her parents for one year, before moving to Rooty Hill.
